title: Quantum statistical mechanics: Gauge invariance, operator shifting, hyperdensity functionals, and nonequilibrium sum rules
abstract: We provide an extended acount of the recent statistical mechanical theory of gauge invariance against operator shifting in quantum many-body systems (arXiv:2509.20494). The gauge transformation is enacted by a shifting superoperator that displaces the fundamental position and momentum degrees of freedom. The shifting superoperator constitutes a map between Hilbert space operators and it features Lie algebra commutator structure. Averages of general observables remain invariant under the shifting both in and out of thermal equilibrium, as well as in groundstates. The gauge invariance induces exact sum rules that interconnect global observables and associated locally resolved correlation functions. In particular we describe the resulting one-body force, hyperforce, product, and two-body sum rules. We relate the shifting superoperator to a previously formulated quantum canonical transformation and present the generalization of quantum shifting to multi-component systems. The gauge theory respects fundamental fermionic and bosonic particle properties, as we demonstrate by proving the compatibility of operator shifting and exchange symmetry. We formulate the quantum version of hyperdensity functional theory to provide formal access to hyperforces as well as to general averaged quantum observables via universal density functionals. For time-dependent situations, we describe quantum dynamical gauge invariance and prove exact dynamical sum rules for nonequilibrium situations, as generated by Hamiltonian time dependence. We argue for the fundamental status of statistical mechanical gauge invariance based on the compliance of the underlying geometry with canonical quantization according to Dirac's correspondence principle. Analogies and differences of the quantum mechanical sum rules with their classical counterparts remain indicative of the respective levels of description.
summary: This paper establishes a formal gauge theory for quantum statistical mechanics based on the invariance of observables under operator shifting. It introduces a 'hyperdensity functional theory' and derives exact sum rules that connect local correlations to global observables. The framework is applicable to both equilibrium and time-dependent quantum many-body systems.
main_problem: Developing a generalized statistical mechanical theory of gauge invariance against operator shifting in quantum many-body systems and establishing its connection to hyperdensity functional theory.
main_result: The authors prove that a shifting superoperator induces exact sum rules (force, hyperforce, and two-body) and demonstrate that this gauge invariance is compatible with both particle exchange symmetry and canonical quantization.
method: The framework utilizes superoperator algebra, the Mori product (inner product in Liouville space), and Lie theory to implement gauge transformations via a shifting field.
model_or_system: Quantum many-body systems of N fermions or bosons in thermal equilibrium, non-equilibrium, or ground states, described by a Hamiltonian with kinetic energy, interparticle interaction, and external potentials.
key_observables: Density, current density, force density, hyperforce density, hyperfluctuation profile, and hyperobservables.
important_parameters: Temperature (T), chemical potential (mu), particle mass (m), and the shifting field (epsilon).
main_assumptions: The Hamiltonian is assumed to be additive for the reduction of many-body averages; the shifting field is assumed to be smooth; the system is initially thermalized for the nonequilibrium extension.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from defining the microscopic model and equilibrium gauge invariance to describing operator shifting properties, developing hyperdensity functional theory, extending to nonequilibrium dynamics, and finally relating the quantum theory to its classical counterpart via Dirac's correspondence principle.
why_interesting: It provides a rigorous mathematical foundation for deriving exact sum rules and density functionals, which are essential for understanding many-body dynamics and developing new consistency checks for numerical many-body simulations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
